title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Ethics of Facial Recognition Technology

texts.A: The deployment of facial recognition in public spaces is an unacceptable infringement on the right to anonymity. Unlike a physical ID, your face cannot be left at home. This technology turns every citizen into a permanent suspect, tracked by invisible algorithms [37]. While proponents argue it aids in catching criminals, the cost to our civil liberties is far too high. We are sleepwalking into a surveillance state where the 'chilling effect' on free assembly and protest will be irreversible. [38]A total ban on its use by law enforcement is the only way to protect the democratic fabric of our society.[/38]
texts.B: Facial recognition is a powerful tool for public safety that has been unfairly demonized. In an era of globalized threats, the ability to identify known dangerous individuals in a crowd is a vital necessity [40]. I disagree with the notion that it inherently destroys privacy; rather, it modernizes it. When used with strict judicial oversight, it is no different from any other forensic tool. The focus should be on 'algorithmic auditing' to ensure accuracy across all demographics [39], not on banning a technology that can prevent tragedies before they occur.
texts.C: I share the view that the 'black box' nature of these algorithms is a major concern, as they often exhibit significant racial and gender bias [39]. However, I disagree that a total ban is the solution. We should implement a 'moratorium'—a temporary halt—until international standards for data protection and accuracy are established. I also share the concern about the 'chilling effect' on public life [37], but I believe that with clear 'no-go zones' for the technology, we can balance security with the freedom of the streets.
texts.D: The real danger of facial recognition is not just the surveillance, but the 'predictive policing' it enables. When combined with big data, it attempts to judge a person's intent before they have even acted. I share the view that this technology is currently a threat to public safety due to its technical flaws [40]. However, we must recognize that the private sector's use of this tech—in our phones and banks—is already ubiquitous. The focus must be on 'data sovereignty,' giving individuals the power to opt-out and delete their biometric templates from any database [38].

questions.question: Which expert shares A's concern about the 'chilling effect' of facial recognition on public behavior?
questions.explanation: Expert A mentions the 'chilling effect' on free assembly. Expert C explicitly states: 'I also share the concern about the chilling effect on public life.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on the best way to handle personal biometric data?
questions.explanation: Expert D suggests 'data sovereignty' and 'opt-out' options. Expert A, however, argues for a 'total ban,' implying that individual control is insufficient to prevent the surveillance state.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to B regarding the need for technical accuracy and auditing?
questions.explanation: Expert B calls for 'algorithmic auditing' to ensure accuracy. Expert C also notes that algorithms 'often exhibit significant... bias' and calls for standards of accuracy.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the current reliability or necessity of the technology for law enforcement?
questions.explanation: Expert B calls it a 'vital necessity.' Expert D argues it is 'currently a threat to public safety due to its technical flaws.'
